title: Linux 7.1 Nears i486 CPU Support Removal, Phoronix Reports Upcoming Kernel Change
body:
A Long-Awaited Shift: Linux 7.1 Moves to Ditch Support for Aging i486 CPUs

In a significant step forward for the evolution of the Linux operating system, developers have begun laying the groundwork to remove support for the venerable Intel 486 CPU. This decision marks the culmination of a long-standing effort to modernize the Linux kernel, which has been running on a wide range of hardware configurations for over two decades. As the Linux 7.1 merge window approaches, the inclusion of a crucial patch signals the start of a phased withdrawal of support for this aging CPU architecture.

This move is not a surprise, but rather a natural progression of the Linux kernel's development trajectory. As new hardware platforms and features emerge, the kernel must adapt to ensure continued compatibility and performance. Intel 486 CPUs, first released in 1989, are now woefully outdated and no longer meet the demands of modern computing. Their removal will not only streamline the kernel but also pave the way for more efficient and secure code development.

The decision to end i486 support also reflects the increasing complexity of maintaining a broad range of hardware configurations. With the rise of more modern CPU architectures, such as x86-64 and ARM, the Linux kernel has expanded to accommodate these newer platforms. By focusing on newer hardware, the kernel can now devote more resources to improving performance, security, and feature sets. This shift will ultimately benefit users, as they will gain access to more efficient and reliable operating system updates.

However, this move will likely have a significant impact on users still relying on older hardware, particularly those running legacy systems within industrial, scientific, or educational settings. In order to ensure a smooth transition, Linux developers will need to carefully manage the removal of i486 support, providing adequate support and alternatives for users who require continued compatibility with older platforms.

The removal of i486 support also raises questions about the Linux kernel's broader compatibility goals. Will this move signal a more concerted effort to prioritize support for newer hardware, potentially at the expense of older configurations? As Linux 7.1 approaches, it will be crucial to monitor the kernel's development trajectory and assess how this change affects the broader Linux community.

As the Linux 7.1 kernel takes shape, this decision represents a significant milestone in the operating system's ongoing evolution. By embracing change and adapting to the needs of modern computing, Linux developers are poised to deliver a faster, more secure, and more efficient kernel that will continue to meet the demands of users worldwide. With continued innovation and development, Linux will remain a leading force in the world of open-source operating systems, driving progress and pushing the boundaries of what is possible.
